subtopics.title: Definition and Branches of Agriculture
common_misconceptions: Nigeria doesn't need agriculture because of oil
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Agriculture contributes about 23% of Nigeria's GDP and employs ~70% of the population. Before oil (pre-1970s), agriculture was the mainstay (groundnut pyramids in Kano, cocoa in West, palm oil in East, cotton in North). Current challenges: climate change, Fulani herdsmen conflicts, inadequate storage, poor funding. Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development. Programs: Anchor Borrowers' Programme, N-Power Agro.

subtopics.title: Importance of Agriculture
common_misconceptions: Only rural people benefit from agriculture
subtopics.teaching_note: Importance: 1) Food security - feeds the nation. 2) Employment - largest employer in Nigeria. 3) Foreign exchange - cocoa, sesame, cashew exports. 4) Raw materials for industries - cotton for textiles, rubber for tyres, timber for furniture. 5) Revenue for government - taxes, export duties. 6) Market for industrial products - fertilizers, tractors, pesticides. 7) Cultural significance - festivals, traditions.

subtopics.title: Agricultural Ecosystems
common_misconceptions: Humans are not part of the ecosystem
subtopics.teaching_note: Agro-ecosystem: interaction between crops/animals and their environment. Biotic factors: living (plants, animals, microbes, pests, pollinators). Abiotic factors: non-living (rainfall, temperature, soil, light, wind). Food chain: producer → primary consumer → secondary consumer → tertiary consumer. Energy flow: unidirectional, 10% energy transfer between trophic levels (Lindeman's 10% law). Pyramids: number, biomass, energy. Pest management: integrated pest management (IPM) combines biological, cultural, chemical methods.

subtopics.title: Soil Science
common_misconceptions: All plants need the same soil type
subtopics.teaching_note: Soil profile: O (organic), A (topsoil), B (subsoil), C (parent material), R (bedrock). Texture: sand (>0.05mm), silt (0.002-0.05mm), clay (<0.002mm). Loam = ideal mixture. Structure: how particles aggregate (granular, platy, blocky, prismatic, columnar). pH: acidic (<7), neutral (=7), alkaline (>7). Most crops prefer pH 6-7. Macronutrients: N, P, K (primary); Ca, Mg, S (secondary). Micronutrients: Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl. Fertilizers: organic (manure, compost) vs inorganic (NPK, urea).

subtopics.title: Classification of Crops
common_misconceptions: Cash crops are not food crops (some are both)
All tubers are roots (some are stems)
Cereals and legumes are the same
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Major food crops: maize, rice, sorghum, millet, yam, cassava, cocoyam, plantain, beans. Cash crops: cocoa (Ondo, Ogun, Ekiti), palm oil (South-East/South-South), groundnut (North), cotton (North), rubber (Edo, Delta), sesame. Cereals: grass family, staple grains. Legumes: nitrogen-fixing, protein-rich. Tubers: underground storage organs. Classification by life cycle: annuals (complete in one season), biennials (two seasons), perennials (live many years).

subtopics.title: Farming Systems and Practices
common_misconceptions: Shifting cultivation is sustainable in modern Nigeria (population pressure makes it unsustainable)
Organic farming means no pest control
subtopics.teaching_note: Farming systems: Shifting cultivation (slash and burn, move when soil exhausted) - common in rainforests but becoming unsustainable. Bush fallowing: land left to rest 3-5 years. Crop rotation: growing different crops in sequence on same land (legumes → cereals → root crops). Mixed farming: crops + livestock. Mono cropping: single crop (plantation). Inter cropping: two or more crops together (maize + beans). Relay cropping: planting next crop before harvesting current one. Mechanization: tractors, harvesters, irrigation. Nigerian challenge: low mechanization rate (~5%).
